sourceRefs.fallbackText: This PEP documents the semantics and conventions associated with Python docstrings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This PEP documents the semantics and conventions associated with Python docstrings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The aim of this PEP is to standardize the high-level structure of docstrings: what they should contain, and how to say it (without touching on any markup syntax within docstrings). The PEP contains conventions, not laws or syntax.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The aim of this PEP is to standardize the high-level structure of docstrings: what they should contain, and how to say it (without touching on any markup syntax within docstrings). The PEP contains conventions, not laws

sourceRefs.fallbackText: “A universal convention supplies all of maintainability, clarity, consistency, and a foundation for good programming habits too. What it doesn’t do is insist that you follow it against your will. That’s Python!”
sourceRefs.textQuote: “A universal convention supplies all of maintainability, clarity, consistency, and a foundation for good programming habits too. What it doesn’t do is insist that you follow it against your will. That’s Python!”

sourceRefs.fallbackText: —Tim Peters on comp.lang.python, 2001-06-16
sourceRefs.textQuote: —Tim Peters on comp.lang.python, 2001-06-16

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you violate these conventions, the worst you’ll get is some dirty looks. But some software (such as the Docutils docstring processing system PEP 256 , PEP 258 ) will be aware of the conventions, so following them will get you the best results.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you violate these conventions, the worst you’ll get is some dirty looks. But some software (such as the Docutils docstring processing system PEP 256 , PEP 258 ) will be aware of the conventions, so following them will

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A docstring is a string literal that occurs as the first statement in a module, function, class, or method definition. Such a docstring becomes the __doc__ special attribute of that object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: A docstring is a string literal that occurs as the first statement in a module, function, class, or method definition. Such a docstring becomes the __doc__ special attribute of that object.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: All modules should normally have docstrings, and all functions and classes exported by a module should also have docstrings. Public methods (including the __init__ constructor) should also have docstrings. A package may be documented in the module docstring of the __init__.py file in the package directory.
sourceRefs.textQuote: All modules should normally have docstrings, and all functions and classes exported by a module should also have docstrings. Public methods (including the __init__ constructor) should also have docstrings. A package may

sourceRefs.fallbackText: String literals occurring elsewhere in Python code may also act as documentation. They are not recognized by the Python bytecode compiler and are not accessible as runtime object attributes (i.e. not assigned to __doc__ ), but two types of extra docstrings may be extracted by software tools:
sourceRefs.textQuote: String literals occurring elsewhere in Python code may also act as documentation. They are not recognized by the Python bytecode compiler and are not accessible as runtime object attributes (i.e. not assigned to __doc__

sourceRefs.fallbackText: String literals occurring immediately after a simple assignment at the top level of a module, class, or __init__ method are called “attribute docstrings”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: String literals occurring immediately after a simple assignment at the top level of a module, class, or __init__ method are called “attribute docstrings”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: String literals occurring immediately after another docstring are called “additional docstrings”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: String literals occurring immediately after another docstring are called “additional docstrings”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Please see PEP 258 , “Docutils Design Specification”, for a detailed description of attribute and additional docstrings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Please see PEP 258 , “Docutils Design Specification”, for a detailed description of attribute and additional docstrings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For consistency, always use """triple double quotes""" around docstrings. Use r"""raw triple double quotes""" if you use any backslashes in your docstrings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: For consistency, always use """triple double quotes""" around docstrings. Use r"""raw triple double quotes""" if you use any backslashes in your docstrings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are two forms of docstrings: one-liners and multi-line docstrings.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are two forms of docstrings: one-liners and multi-line docstrings.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: One-liners are for really obvious cases. They should really fit on one line. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: One-liners are for really obvious cases. They should really fit on one line. For example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def kos_root (): """Return the pathname of the KOS root directory.""" global _kos_root if _kos_root : return _kos_root ...
sourceRefs.textQuote: def kos_root (): """Return the pathname of the KOS root directory.""" global _kos_root if _kos_root : return _kos_root ...

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Triple quotes are used even though the string fits on one line. This makes it easy to later expand it.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Triple quotes are used even though the string fits on one line. This makes it easy to later expand it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The closing quotes are on the same line as the opening quotes. This looks better for one-liners.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The closing quotes are on the same line as the opening quotes. This looks better for one-liners.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There’s no blank line either before or after the docstring.
sourceRefs.textQuote: There’s no blank line either before or after the docstring.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring is a phrase ending in a period. It prescribes the function or method’s effect as a command (“Do this”, “Return that”), not as a description; e.g. don’t write “Returns the pathname …”.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring is a phrase ending in a period. It prescribes the function or method’s effect as a command (“Do this”, “Return that”), not as a description; e.g. don’t write “Returns the pathname …”.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The one-line docstring should NOT be a “signature” reiterating the function/method parameters (which can be obtained by introspection). Don’t do:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The one-line docstring should NOT be a “signature” reiterating the function/method parameters (which can be obtained by introspection). Don’t do:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def function ( a , b ): """function(a, b) -> list"""
sourceRefs.textQuote: def function ( a , b ): """function(a, b) -> list"""

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This type of docstring is only appropriate for C functions (such as built-ins), where introspection is not possible. However, the nature of the return value cannot be determined by introspection, so it should be mentioned. The preferred form for such a docstring would be something like:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This type of docstring is only appropriate for C functions (such as built-ins), where introspection is not possible. However, the nature of the return value cannot be determined by introspection, so it should be mentione

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def function ( a , b ): """Do X and return a list."""
sourceRefs.textQuote: def function ( a , b ): """Do X and return a list."""

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (Of course “Do X” should be replaced by a useful description!)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (Of course “Do X” should be replaced by a useful description!)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Multi-line docstrings consist of a summary line just like a one-line docstring, followed by a blank line, followed by a more elaborate description. The summary line may be used by automatic indexing tools; it is important that it fits on one line and is separated from the rest of the docstring by a blank line. The summary line may be on the same line as the
sourceRefs.textQuote: Multi-line docstrings consist of a summary line just like a one-line docstring, followed by a blank line, followed by a more elaborate description. The summary line may be used by automatic indexing tools; it is importan

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Insert a blank line after all docstrings (one-line or multi-line) that document a class – generally speaking, the class’s methods are separated from each other by a single blank line, and the docstring needs to be offset from the first method by a blank line.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Insert a blank line after all docstrings (one-line or multi-line) that document a class – generally speaking, the class’s methods are separated from each other by a single blank line, and the docstring needs to be offset

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring of a script (a stand-alone program) should be usable as its “usage” message, printed when the script is invoked with incorrect or missing arguments (or perhaps with a “-h” option, for “help”). Such a docstring should document the script’s function and command line syntax, environment variables, and files. Usage messages can be fairly elaborate
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring of a script (a stand-alone program) should be usable as its “usage” message, printed when the script is invoked with incorrect or missing arguments (or perhaps with a “-h” option, for “help”). Such a docstr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring for a module should generally list the classes, exceptions and functions (and any other objects) that are exported by the module, with a one-line summary of each. (These summaries generally give less detail than the summary line in the object’s docstring.) The docstring for a package (i.e., the docstring of the package’s __init__.py module) sho
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring for a module should generally list the classes, exceptions and functions (and any other objects) that are exported by the module, with a one-line summary of each. (These summaries generally give less detail

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring for a function or method should summarize its behavior and document its arguments, return value(s), side effects, exceptions raised, and restrictions on when it can be called (all if applicable). Optional arguments should be indicated. It should be documented whether keyword arguments are part of the interface.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring for a function or method should summarize its behavior and document its arguments, return value(s), side effects, exceptions raised, and restrictions on when it can be called (all if applicable). Optional a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring for a class should summarize its behavior and list the public methods and instance variables. If the class is intended to be subclassed, and has an additional interface for subclasses, this interface should be listed separately (in the docstring). The class constructor should be documented in the docstring for its __init__ method. Individual me
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring for a class should summarize its behavior and list the public methods and instance variables. If the class is intended to be subclassed, and has an additional interface for subclasses, this interface should

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a class subclasses another class and its behavior is mostly inherited from that class, its docstring should mention this and summarize the differences. Use the verb “override” to indicate that a subclass method replaces a superclass method and does not call the superclass method; use the verb “extend” to indicate that a subclass method calls the superclas
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a class subclasses another class and its behavior is mostly inherited from that class, its docstring should mention this and summarize the differences. Use the verb “override” to indicate that a subclass method replac

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Do not use the Emacs convention of mentioning the arguments of functions or methods in upper case in running text. Python is case sensitive and the argument names can be used for keyword arguments, so the docstring should document the correct argument names. It is best to list each argument on a separate line. For example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Do not use the Emacs convention of mentioning the arguments of functions or methods in upper case in running text. Python is case sensitive and the argument names can be used for keyword arguments, so the docstring shoul

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def complex ( real = 0.0 , imag = 0.0 ): """Form a complex number. Keyword arguments: real -- the real part (default 0.0) imag -- the imaginary part (default 0.0) """ if imag == 0.0 and real == 0.0 : return complex_zero ...
sourceRefs.textQuote: def complex ( real = 0.0 , imag = 0.0 ): """Form a complex number. Keyword arguments: real -- the real part (default 0.0) imag -- the imaginary part (default 0.0) """ if imag == 0.0 and real == 0.0 : return complex_zero

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unless the entire docstring fits on a line, place the closing quotes on a line by themselves. This way, Emacs’ fill-paragraph command can be used on it.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unless the entire docstring fits on a line, place the closing quotes on a line by themselves. This way, Emacs’ fill-paragraph command can be used on it.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Docstring processing tools will strip a uniform amount of indentation from the second and further lines of the docstring, equal to the minimum indentation of all non-blank lines after the first line. Any indentation in the first line of the docstring (i.e., up to the first newline) is insignificant and removed. Relative indentation of later lines in the docs
sourceRefs.textQuote: Docstring processing tools will strip a uniform amount of indentation from the second and further lines of the docstring, equal to the minimum indentation of all non-blank lines after the first line. Any indentation in t

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Since code is much more precise than words, here is an implementation of the algorithm:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Since code is much more precise than words, here is an implementation of the algorithm:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def trim ( docstring ): if not docstring : return '' # Convert tabs to spaces (following the normal Python rules) # and split into a list of lines: lines = docstring . expandtabs () . splitlines () # Determine minimum indentation (first line doesn't count): indent = sys . maxsize for line in lines [ 1 :]: stripped = line . lstrip () if stripped : indent = mi
sourceRefs.textQuote: def trim ( docstring ): if not docstring : return '' # Convert tabs to spaces (following the normal Python rules) # and split into a list of lines: lines = docstring . expandtabs () . splitlines () # Determine minimum in

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The docstring in this example contains two newline characters and is therefore 3 lines long. The first and last lines are blank:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The docstring in this example contains two newline characters and is therefore 3 lines long. The first and last lines are blank:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def foo (): """ This is the second line of the docstring. """
sourceRefs.textQuote: def foo (): """ This is the second line of the docstring. """

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> print repr ( foo . __doc__ ) '\n This is the second line of the docstring.\n ' >>> foo . __doc__ . splitlines () ['', ' This is the second line of the docstring.', ' '] >>> trim ( foo . __doc__ ) 'This is the second line of the docstring.'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> print repr ( foo . __doc__ ) '\n This is the second line of the docstring.\n ' >>> foo . __doc__ . splitlines () ['', ' This is the second line of the docstring.', ' '] >>> trim ( foo . __doc__ ) 'This is the second

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Once trimmed, these docstrings are equivalent:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Once trimmed, these docstrings are equivalent:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: def foo (): """A multi-line docstring. """ def bar (): """ A multi-line docstring. """
sourceRefs.textQuote: def foo (): """A multi-line docstring. """ def bar (): """ A multi-line docstring. """
